[ZML](https://zml.ai/), a hot French AI startup [endorsed](https://x.com/ylecun/status/1836030233796874244) by Turing Award winner Yann LeCun, has released [inference](https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/03/artificial-intelligence-definition-glossary-hallucinations-guide-to-common-ai-terms/#inference)-performance software that allows [a variety of open-source large language models](https://x.com/steeve/status/2073166739668345255) to run on a [variety of chips](https://x.com/steeve/status/2058815921997680841) — including Nvidia’s, AMD’s, Google’s TPU, Apple Metal and Intel Arc. With [ZML/LLMD](https://zml.ai/llmd/), the newly launched LLM inference server, the company’s ambition is to break existing silos and make different chips available for AI use cases at their maximum available speed, and sometimes faster, ZML founder Steeve Morin told TechCrunch. As AI becomes integrated into our work and everyday lives, optimizing inference — aka, the processing of prompts — has been outpacing [model training](https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/03/artificial-intelligence-definition-glossary-hallucinations-guide-to-common-ai-terms/#training) in importance, but often feels patchy behind the scenes, with software and architecture barriers that lead to vendor lock-in, Morin said. The promise of achieving peak performance across a variety of chips is a technological feat, but it could also be a market disruptor, amid mounting fears over AI-related costs. ZML hopes to provide enterprises and clouds with the option to use a mix of chips, some of which might be less costly or consume less energy. “The idea is to give people back the power to create their own system and achieve real efficiency gains that allow [AI] to be disseminated,” Morin said. Such a software assist may help novel AI chipmakers, many of which happen to be from Europe, Morin observed, citing [Axelera](https://axelera.ai/), [Fractile](https://www.fractile.ai/), [Kalray](https://www.kalrayinc.com/), [OLIX](https://olix.com/), [Q.ANT](https://qant.com/), [SiPearl](https://sipearl.com/), [SpiNNcloud](https://spinncloud.com/), and [VSORA](https://vsora.com/). But more than their region of origin, what matters to him is that ZML can work with them on “things that haven’t been done before anywhere in the world.” That doesn’t mean Morin is bearish on Nvidia. [He’s not](https://www.thetwentyminutevc.com/steeve-morin), in part because of its existing supply. He told Tec...

Sysdig says the JadePuffer group used an autonomous AI agent to plan and execute an end-to-end ransomware campaign, adapting in real time, stealing credentials and encrypting data while exposing flaws in its financial understanding [Raphael Kahan](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Raphael_Kahan "Raphael Kahan")| Print[Find an error? Report us](//ynet.co.il/redmail?notifyMistake=rjms4kn7ze "Find an error? Report us") Related Topics [Artificial Intelligence](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Artificial_Intelligence "Artificial Intelligence") [Cybersecurity](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Cybersecurity "Cybersecurity") [Cyberattack](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Cyberattack "Cyberattack") [AI](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/AI "AI") Security researchers have identified what they say is the first documented case of a ransomware attack planned, managed and executed entirely by an autonomous AI agent, marking a potentially significant shift in the global cyber threat landscape. The attack group, known as JadePuffer, used a large language model to manage every stage of the campaign, from initial intelligence gathering and credential theft to lateral movement inside the network, data encryption and the ransom demand, according to a report by cloud security company Sysdig. The most concerning capability demonstrated in the attack, according to the report, was the AI agent’s flexibility and ability to adapt in real time. Like a skilled human hacker, the autonomous system was able to identify failures in its own actions and correct them quickly. In one documented case, the system encountered a login error, analyzed the problem, changed the code parameters and produced a working solution within 31 seconds. The initial breach was carried out by exploiting a known vulnerability, CVE-2025-3248, in Langflow, a popular open-source framework used to build AI applications. After gaining access, the AI agent scanned databases, extracted passwords and encryption keys, and began moving through the organization’s network toward production servers running Alibaba Nacos. At that stage, the agent encrypted 1,342 service configuration items, deleted the originals and left behind a text file containing a ransom demand in Bitcoin and a contact address. ### ‘This is only the beginning’ Despite the sophistication of the aut...

highlights.titleZh: Agentic AI Liability Fuels Issues Reaching Beyond the Law’s Edge - Bloomberg Law News
highlights.content: [Business & Practice](/business-and-practice/) [Email](mailto:?subject=News%20from%20Bloomberg%20Industry%20Group%3A%20Business%20%26%20Practice%3A%20Agentic%20AI%20Liability%20Fuels%20Issues%20Reaching%20Beyond%20the%20Law%E2%80%99s%20Edge&body=Here%20is%20a%20story%20that%20I%20thought%20would%20interest%20you.%0D%0A%0D%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fnews.bloomberglaw.com%2Flegal-exchange-insights-and-commentary%2Fagentic-ai-liability-fuels-issues-reaching-beyond-the-laws-edge%3Futm_source%3DEmail_Share) Share To:[Facebook](https://facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fnews.bloomberglaw.com%2Flegal-exchange-insights-and-commentary%2Fagentic-ai-liability-fuels-issues-reaching-beyond-the-laws-edge) [LinkedIn](https://linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https%3A%2F%2Fnews.bloomberglaw.com%2Flegal-exchange-insights-and-commentary%2Fagentic-ai-liability-fuels-issues-reaching-beyond-the-laws-edge) Perspectives # Agentic AI Liability Fuels Issues Reaching Beyond the Law’s Edge July 8, 2026, 8:30 AM UTC Mark Kelley MoloLamken ## The Bottom Line * As AI systems become more autonomous and take actions across the web, courts will confront the scope of those systems’ authority under the law of agency. * Liability allocation will grow complex as courts navigate whether and to what degree the AI developer, the AI deployer, or the AI user are liable for harm. * The latest generation of cases involve AI systems that conduct extended, autonomous interactions and make independent decisions, raising the question of what it means for an AI to have acted at all. In the fall of 2025, Amazon’s security team noticed something unusual: An AI-powered browser called Comet, built by a startup called Perplexity AI, was logging into Amazon customer accounts, browsing products, and completing purchases. When Amazon engineers deployed software to block it, Perplexity updated its browser to evade detection. Amazon sent cease-and-desist letters. Perplexity published a defiant blog post titled “Bullying is Not Innovation.” The same day, the matter was in federal court. [*Amazon.com Services LLC v. Perplexity AI, Inc*](https://www.bloomberglaw.com/product/blaw/document/X1Q6OTA436O2?criteria_id=3445384877d3aae2c61a31cce4b6d9d8&searchGuid=fed32da8-359b-427a-ba7a-31aabb9b9397)*.*, may be the first federal lawsuit directly implicating a commercially deployed agentic artificial intelligence system. Although litigated primarily on [computer fraud grounds](https://www.bloomberglaw.com/public/document/AmazoncomServicesLLCvPerplexityAIIncDocketNo325cv09514NDCalNov042?doc_id=X7KPE0E8ORL94MOPV85TVF0INP2), the case hints at an important, undeveloped area of law — agentic AI and the law of agency. As AI systems become capable of autonomous action across the web — browsing, negotiating, purchasing — courts will confront the scope of those systems’ authority under the law of agency and determine where liability falls. To do so, they’ll need to reexamine some of the foundational legal premises of agency. ## Agentic AI & Agency Law Conventional AI systems are reactive: A user submits a prompt (a question), the system produces an output (an answer), and the interaction ends. By contrast, an agentic system can receive a high-level objective — for example, “book the cheapest flight to London next Thursday” — and break that objective into discrete tasks, select and use tools, interact with external systems, evaluate results, and execute without further input. Three characteristics define agentic AI. First, agentic systems engage in autonomous planning. Second, they integrate with external systems and take actions with real consequences. And third, they operate continuously over extended periods, potentially executing hundreds of actions before a human reviews the results. For its part, the common law of agency rests on familiar foundations involving an agent, a principal, and mutual consent. Liability flows predictably: A principal is bound by acts of agents within the scope of their authorit...

highlights.content: [Asosiy kontentga oʻtish](#main) * [Uzbekistan](/en/uzbekistan/) * [World](/en/world/) * [Society](/en/society/) * [Tech](/en/technology/) * [Sport](/en/sport/) * [Auto](/en/auto/) * [Culture](/en/culture/) * [Economy](/en/economy/) * [Ўзбекча](#) * [O'zbek](#) * [Русский](#) * [Türkçe](#) * [Қазақша](#) * [Français](#) * [Español](#) * [Deutsch](#) # French startup ZML challenges NVIDIA's hegemony in the AI chip market ·1·[Technology](/en/technology/) In an era of rapid AI development, [NVIDIA](/en/tags/nvidia/)'s absolute market dominance is facing a serious challenge. French startup ZML has introduced new software backed by Turing Award winner Yann LeCun. This product allows AI models to run at maximum speed on chips from various manufacturers. This was reported by Techcrunch.com [reports](https://zamin.uz/index.php?do=go&url=aHR0cHM6Ly90ZWNoY3J1bmNoLmNvbS8yMDI2LzA3LzA4L2hvdC1mcmVuY2gtc3RhcnR1cC16bWwtcmVsZWFzZXMtZnJlZS1wcm9kdWN0LXRvLXNwZWVkLWluZmVyZW5jZS1hY3Jvc3MtbG90cy1vZi1haS1jaGlwcy8%3D) . According to TechCrunch, the new inference server called ZML/LLMD is designed to run open-source large language models (LLM) efficiently on various processors, not just NVIDIA, but also AMD, Google TPU, Apple Metal, and Intel Arc. This [technology](/en/tags/tehnologiya/) allows companies to avoid vendor lock-in and fully utilize existing hardware resources. ## A revolution in the inference process Currently, using ready-made AI models, i.e., the inference process, is becoming more important than training AI. ZML founder Steeve Morin explains that many companies are dependent on specific [chip](/en/tags/chip/) manufacturers due to software and architectural barriers. The new platform removes these barriers and unlocks the maximum potential of the chips. This solution is particularly relevant given the rising costs and energy consumption of AI. The ZML startup enables enterprises to use combinations of cheaper or more energy-efficient chips. This, in turn, makes the implementation of AI systems more accessible and economically efficient. ## New opportunities for European chips The software introduced by ZML is expected to be a major boost not only for giants but also for emerging chip manufacturers. In his interview, Morin listed several promising companies: * Axelera and Fractile; * Kalray and OLIX; * SiPearl and SpiNNcloud; * Q.ANT and VSORA. The startup leader emphasized that his relationship with NVIDIA is good and that this project is not against them, but rather serves to expand the market. Nevertheless, ZML currently faces competition from major players like Baseten, Inferact, and RadixArk. The Paris-based team of just 20 people has now reached the stage of co-designing silicon. This news is also important for specialists and local IT companies. Open-source models running on various chips allow for the optimization of computing power and cost reduction. This step by the ZML startup signals the beginning of an 'inference gold rush' in the AI market. highlights.titleZh: French startup ZML challenges NVIDIA's hegemony in the AI chip market - Zamin.uz
highlights.content: [Asosiy kontentga oʻtish](#main) * [Uzbekistan](/en/uzbekistan/) * [World](/en/world/) * [Society](/en/society/) * [Tech](/en/technology/) * [Sport](/en/sport/) * [Auto](/en/auto/) * [Culture](/en/culture/) * [Economy](/en/economy/) * [Ўзбекча](#) * [O'zbek](#) * [Русский](#) * [Türkçe](#) * [Қазақша](#) * [Français](#) * [Español](#) * [Deutsch](#) # French startup ZML challenges NVIDIA's hegemony in the AI chip market ·1·[Technology](/en/technology/) In an era of rapid AI development, [NVIDIA](/en/tags/nvidia/)'s absolute market dominance is facing a serious challenge. French startup ZML has introduced new software backed by Turing Award winner Yann LeCun. This product allows AI models to run at maximum speed on chips from various manufacturers. This was reported by Techcrunch.com [reports](https://zamin.uz/index.php?do=go&url=aHR0cHM6Ly90ZWNoY3J1bmNoLmNvbS8yMDI2LzA3LzA4L2hvdC1mcmVuY2gtc3RhcnR1cC16bWwtcmVsZWFzZXMtZnJlZS1wcm9kdWN0LXRvLXNwZWVkLWluZmVyZW5jZS1hY3Jvc3MtbG90cy1vZi1haS1jaGlwcy8%3D) . According to TechCrunch, the new inference server called ZML/LLMD is designed to run open-source large language models (LLM) efficiently on various processors, not just NVIDIA, but also AMD, Google TPU, Apple Metal, and Intel Arc. This [technology](/en/tags/tehnologiya/) allows companies to avoid vendor lock-in and fully utilize existing hardware resources. ## A revolution in the inference process Currently, using ready-made AI models, i.e., the inference process, is becoming more important than training AI. ZML founder Steeve Morin explains that many companies are dependent on specific [chip](/en/tags/chip/) manufacturers due to software and architectural barriers. The new platform removes these barriers and unlocks the maximum potential of the chips. This solution is particularly relevant given the rising costs and energy consumption of AI. The ZML startup enables enterprises to use combinations of cheaper or more energy-efficient chips. This, in turn, makes the implementation of AI systems more accessible and economically efficient. ## New opportunities for European chips The software introduced by ZML is expected to be a major boost not only for giants but also for emerging chip manufacturers. In his interview, Morin listed several promising companies: * Axelera and Fractile; * Kalray and OLIX; * SiPearl and SpiNNcloud; * Q.ANT and VSORA. The startup leader emphasized that his relationship with NVIDIA is good and that this project is not against them, but rather serves to expand the market. Nevertheless, ZML currently faces competition from major players like Baseten, Inferact, and RadixArk. The Paris-based team of just 20 people has now reached the stage of co-designing silicon. This news is also important for specialists and local IT companies. Open-source models running on various chips allow for the optimization of computing power and cost reduction. This step by the ZML startup signals the beginning of an 'inference gold rush' in the AI market. highlights.content: ### Topics [Biotech & Health](/category/biotech-health/) [Cloud Computing](/tag/cloud-computing/) [Crypto](/category/cryptocurrency/) [Enterprise](/category/enterprise/) [EVs](/tag/evs/) [Fintech](/category/fintech/) [Fundraising](/category/fundraising/) [Gadgets](/category/gadgets/) [Gaming](/category/gaming/) [Google](/tag/google/) [Government & Policy](/category/government-policy/) [Hardware](/category/hardware/) [Instagram](/tag/instagram/) [Layoffs](/tag/layoffs/) [Media & Entertainment](/category/media-entertainment/) [Meta](/tag/meta/) [Microsoft](/tag/microsoft/) [Privacy](/category/privacy/) [Robotics](/category/robotics/) [Security](/category/security/) [Social](/category/social/) [Space](/category/space/) [Startups](/category/startups/) [TikTok](/tag/tiktok/) [Transportation](/category/transportation/) [Venture](/category/venture/) ### More from TechCrunch [Staff](/about-techcrunch/) [Events](/events/) [Startup Battlefield](/startup-battlefield/) [StrictlyVC](https://strictlyvc.com/) [Newsletters](/newsletters/) [Podcasts](/podcasts/) [Videos](/video/) [Partner Content](/sponsored/) [TechCrunch Brand Studio](/brand-studio/) [Crunchboard](https://www.crunchboard.com/) [Contact Us](/contact-us/) [AI](https://techcrunch.com/category/artificial-intelligence/) # Hot French startup ZML releases free product to speed inference across lots of AI chips [Anna Heim](https://techcrunch.com/author/anna-heim/) The days of Nvidia’s unparalleled market dominance aren’t over, but challengers and choices are arising from all directions. [ZML](https://zml.ai/), a hot French AI startup [endorsed](https://x.com/ylecun/status/1836030233796874244) by Turing Award winner Yann LeCun, has released [inference](https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/03/artificial-intelligence-definition-glossary-hallucinations-guide-to-common-ai-terms/#inference)-performance software that allows [a variety of open-source large language models](https://x.com/steeve/status/2073166739668345255) to run on a [variety of chips](https://x.com/steeve/status/2058815921997680841) — including Nvidia’s, AMD’s, Google’s TPU, Apple Metal and Intel Arc. With [ZML/LLMD](https://zml.ai/llmd/), the newly launched LLM inference server, the company’s ambition is to break existing silos and make different chips available for AI use cases at their maximum available speed, and sometimes faster, ZML founder Steeve Morin told TechCrunch. As AI becomes integrated into our work and everyday lives, optimizing inference — aka, the processing of prompts — has been outpacing [model training](https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/03/artificial-intelligence-definition-glossary-hallucinations-guide-to-common-ai-terms/#training) in importance, but often feels patchy behind the scenes, with software and architecture barriers that lead to vendor lock-in, Morin said. The promise of achieving peak performance across a variety of chips is a technological feat, but it could also be a market disruptor, amid mounting fears over AI-related costs. ZML hopes to provide enterprises and clouds with the option to use a mix of chips, some of which might be less costly or consume less energy. “The idea is to give people back the power to create their own system and achieve real efficiency gains that allow [AI] to be disseminated,” Morin said. Such a software assist may help novel AI chipmakers, many of which happen to be from Europe, Morin observed, citing [Axelera](https://axelera.ai/), [Fractile](https://www.fractile.ai/), [Kalray](https://www.kalrayinc.com/), [OLIX](https://olix.com/), [Q.ANT](https://qant.com/), [SiPearl](https://sipearl.com/), [SpiNNcloud](https://spinncloud.com/), and [VSORA](https://vsora.com/). But more than their region of origin, what matters to him is that ZML can work with them on “things that haven’t been done before anywhere in the world.” That doesn’t mean Morin is bearish on Nvidia. [He’s not](https://www.thetwentyminutevc.com/steeve-morin), in part because of its existing supply. He told Tec...

highlights.title: Ericsson is starting to look like the only safe bet for RAN chips - Light Reading
highlights.titleZh: Ericsson is starting to look like the only safe bet for RAN chips - Light Reading
highlights.content: * [5G](/wireless/5g) * [Virtualization](/digital-transformation/virtualization) * [Semiconductors](/network-technology/semiconductors) * [AI & Machine Learning](/digital-transformation/ai-machine-learning) * [News](/latest-news) # Ericsson is starting to look like the only safe bet for RAN chips Concerns about using Nvidia's GPUs and Intel's CPUs in the RAN are making it easier for Ericsson to justify its work on custom silicon. [Iain Morris,](/author/iain-morris)International Editor,Light Reading July 8, 2026 9 Min Read (Source: Iain Morris/Light Reading) To the bemusement of many onlookers, Nvidia has been showering the network hoi polloi with dollars like a farmer scattering grain. It has already plowed [$1 billion into Nokia](/5g/nvidia-takes-1b-stake-in-nokia-which-promises-5g-and-6g-overhaul) and another [$2 billion into Marvell Technology](/5g/nvidia-backed-marvell-pitches-one-chip-to-rule-the-ran), which makes connectivity chips for the Finnish vendor. The crop for Nvidia would be an even bigger field of graphics processing units (GPUs), the chips it mainly sells to giant data centers for the training of AI's large language models (LLMs). But skepticism about whether those GPUs will take root in the radio access network (RAN) has not abated. The latest forecasts from a respected analyst seem to vindicate the doubts. Each year, telcos spend about $35 billion on RAN products, according to both Omdia, a Light Reading sister company, and Dell'Oro, another firm that tracks this market. After an initial flurry of spending on 5G network deployment, expenditure is down from a high point of $45 billion in 2022. Neither one of the analyst firms envisages any growth this year. Even worse, though, is Dell'Oro's latest [five-year RAN outlook](https://www.delloro.com/news/ai-ran-to-reach-35-b-over-next-five-years/). Related:[Nvidia has a radical new AI-RAN plan – a 6G radio unit chip](/6g/nvidia-has-a-radical-new-ai-ran-plan-a-6g-radio-unit-chip) Despite much chatter about AI-RAN, the concept promoted by Nvidia, Dell'Oro does not expect it to have a big impact on sales. It will not lead operators to spend more on RAN products than they currently do, [according to Stefan Pongratz](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/spongratz_we-are-in-the-middle-of-updating-all-our-share-7479979977900195840-Pt10/?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAtQMzsBW_GgS-aY6MUcjGHQzXXL1xJ4GSw), a Dell'Oro analyst. Nor will most operators be persuaded to buy GPU-based RAN products for their AI needs, judging by the Dell'Oro forecast. By 2030, what Pongratz calls "GPU RAN" will be a "$1B+ market," said the analyst in his LinkedIn post. The "+" in that statement clearly gives Dell'Oro a get-out clause should GPU RAN unexpectedly mushroom. Predictions can also be revised upward, as Dell'Oro has recently done. But if the analyst company were today more bullish about GPU RAN, it would naturally have set the floor for revenues at a higher level than $1 billion. As an annual figure, and without growth in the overall RAN market, this would equate to less than 3% of total spending. ##### Who needs a GPU? For Nvidia, one issue is that the power-hungry GPUs made famous by generative AI (GenAI) would probably be an expensive misallocation of resources in the RAN, like using a crop duster to water a pot plant. Nvidia appears to have recognized all this and is known to be working on more lightweight GPUs suitable for the baseband units of a RAN, and [even the actual radios](/6g/nvidia-has-a-radical-new-ai-ran-plan-a-6g-radio-unit-chip). "If you do something for that part of the network, the GPU – if it's still a GPU, of course – has very different properties than a GPU like GB300 that you would push to a data center with liquid cooling and all that, so it's already a different platform," said Gabriel Foglander, Ericsson's head of RAN strategy, during [a recent interview](/5g/ericsson-defends-chip-strategy-as-nvidia-plan-rattles-investors). Related:[Ericsson defen...

Report us](//ynet.co.il/redmail?notifyMistake=by11c2oomml "Find an error? Report us") Related Topics [AI](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/AI "AI") [Startup](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Startup "Startup") The rapid growth of generative AI has created one of the fastest-expanding technology markets in history, but Israeli startup Velocity believes the next major challenge will not be building AI products, but finding ways to monetize and distribute them at scale. The company announced Tuesday that it has raised $27 million in seed funding in a round led by NFX and Red Dot Capital Partners, with participation from Stardom Ventures, Corner Ventures and Transcend. Velocity was founded by CEO Tal Shoham alongside COO Amir Shaked and CPO Nimrod Zuta, former executives at ironSource and Unity who spent more than a decade building monetization, advertising and growth infrastructure used by developers worldwide. Velocity is developing infrastructure for AI-native applications as companies face two growing challenges: rising costs of running advanced AI models and difficulty converting large numbers of free users into paying subscribers. While hundreds of millions of people use AI-powered products, including assistants, coding tools and specialized applications, only a small percentage become paying customers. The company argues that traditional subscription models alone may not be enough and is building a monetization layer based on AI-native advertising and recommendations integrated directly into user conversations. Instead of limiting users of an AI photo-editing tool to a small number of free images before requiring payment, Velocity’s technology could allow more free usage by generating revenue from advertisements based on real-time user needs. At the center of the platform is what Velocity calls “user intent” — the information people reveal when they interact with AI systems about what they want to learn, create, buy or solve. Sysdig says the JadePuffer group used an autonomous AI agent to plan and execute an end-to-end ransomware campaign, adapting in real time, stealing credentials and encrypting data while exposing flaws in its financial understanding [Raphael Kahan](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Raphael_Kahan "Raphael Kahan")| Print[Find an error? Report us](//ynet.co.il/redmail?notifyMistake=rjms4kn7ze "Find an error? Report us") Related Topics [Artificial Intelligence](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Artificial_Intelligence "Artificial Intelligence") [Cybersecurity](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Cybersecurity "Cybersecurity") [Cyberattack](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Cyberattack "Cyberattack") [AI](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/AI "AI") Security researchers have identified what they say is the first documented case of a ransomware attack planned, managed and executed entirely by an autonomous AI agent, marking a potentially significant shift in the global cyber threat landscape. The attack group, known as JadePuffer, used a large language model to manage every stage of the campaign, from initial intelligence gathering and credential theft to lateral movement inside the network, data encryption and the ransom demand, according to a report by cloud security company Sysdig. The most concerning capability demonstrated in the attack, according to the report, was the AI agent’s flexibility and ability to adapt in real time. Like a skilled human hacker, the autonomous system was able to identify failures in its own actions and correct them quickly. In one documented case, the system encountered a login error, analyzed the problem, changed the code parameters and produced a working solution within 31 seconds. The initial breach was carried out by exploiting a known vulnerability, CVE-2025-3248, in Langflow, a popular open-source framework used to build AI applications. After gaining access, the AI agent scanned databases, extracted passwords and encryption keys, and began moving through the organization’s network toward production servers running Alibaba Nacos. At that stage, the agent encrypted 1,342 service configuration items, deleted the originals and left behind a text file containing a ransom demand in Bitcoin and a contact address. ### ‘This is only the beginning’ Despite the sophistication of the aut...

AI Agents.content: [Business & Practice](/business-and-practice/) [Email](mailto:?subject=News%20from%20Bloomberg%20Industry%20Group%3A%20Business%20%26%20Practice%3A%20Agentic%20AI%20Liability%20Fuels%20Issues%20Reaching%20Beyond%20the%20Law%E2%80%99s%20Edge&body=Here%20is%20a%20story%20that%20I%20thought%20would%20interest%20you.%0D%0A%0D%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fnews.bloomberglaw.com%2Flegal-exchange-insights-and-commentary%2Fagentic-ai-liability-fuels-issues-reaching-beyond-the-laws-edge%3Futm_source%3DEmail_Share) Share To:[Facebook](https://facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fnews.bloomberglaw.com%2Flegal-exchange-insights-and-commentary%2Fagentic-ai-liability-fuels-issues-reaching-beyond-the-laws-edge) [LinkedIn](https://linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https%3A%2F%2Fnews.bloomberglaw.com%2Flegal-exchange-insights-and-commentary%2Fagentic-ai-liability-fuels-issues-reaching-beyond-the-laws-edge) Perspectives # Agentic AI Liability Fuels Issues Reaching Beyond the Law’s Edge July 8, 2026, 8:30 AM UTC Mark Kelley MoloLamken ## The Bottom Line * As AI systems become more autonomous and take actions across the web, courts will confront the scope of those systems’ authority under the law of agency. * Liability allocation will grow complex as courts navigate whether and to what degree the AI developer, the AI deployer, or the AI user are liable for harm. * The latest generation of cases involve AI systems that conduct extended, autonomous interactions and make independent decisions, raising the question of what it means for an AI to have acted at all. In the fall of 2025, Amazon’s security team noticed something unusual: An AI-powered browser called Comet, built by a startup called Perplexity AI, was logging into Amazon customer accounts, browsing products, and completing purchases. When Amazon engineers deployed software to block it, Perplexity updated its browser to evade detection. Amazon sent cease-and-desist letters. Perplexity published a defiant blog post titled “Bullying is Not Innovation.” The same day, the matter was in federal court. [*Amazon.com Services LLC v. Perplexity AI, Inc*](https://www.bloomberglaw.com/product/blaw/document/X1Q6OTA436O2?criteria_id=3445384877d3aae2c61a31cce4b6d9d8&searchGuid=fed32da8-359b-427a-ba7a-31aabb9b9397)*.*, may be the first federal lawsuit directly implicating a commercially deployed agentic artificial intelligence system. Although litigated primarily on [computer fraud grounds](https://www.bloomberglaw.com/public/document/AmazoncomServicesLLCvPerplexityAIIncDocketNo325cv09514NDCalNov042?doc_id=X7KPE0E8ORL94MOPV85TVF0INP2), the case hints at an important, undeveloped area of law — agentic AI and the law of agency. As AI systems become capable of autonomous action across the web — browsing, negotiating, purchasing — courts will confront the scope of those systems’ authority under the law of agency and determine where liability falls. To do so, they’ll need to reexamine some of the foundational legal premises of agency. ## Agentic AI & Agency Law Conventional AI systems are reactive: A user submits a prompt (a question), the system produces an output (an answer), and the interaction ends. By contrast, an agentic system can receive a high-level objective — for example, “book the cheapest flight to London next Thursday” — and break that objective into discrete tasks, select and use tools, interact with external systems, evaluate results, and execute without further input. Three characteristics define agentic AI. First, agentic systems engage in autonomous planning. Second, they integrate with external systems and take actions with real consequences. And third, they operate continuously over extended periods, potentially executing hundreds of actions before a human reviews the results. For its part, the common law of agency rests on familiar foundations involving an agent, a principal, and mutual consent. Liability flows predictably: A principal is bound by acts of agents within the scope of their authorit...

AI Agents.titleZh: Threat Actors Uses Agentic AI to Rapidly Compromise Cloud Target - Infosecurity Magazine
AI Agents.content: [Infosecurity Magazine Home](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/) » [News](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/) » [Threat Actors Uses Agentic AI to Rapidly Compromise Cloud Target](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/threat-actor-agentic-ai-cloud/) # Threat Actors Uses Agentic AI to Rapidly Compromise Cloud Target [News](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/) ## Written by ### [Phil Muncaster](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/profile/phil-muncaster/) UK / EMEA News Reporter, Infosecurity Magazine * [Email Phil](mailto:phil@pmmediauk.com?subject=Email from Infosecurity Magazine website) * [Follow @philmuncaster](http://twitter.com/philmuncaster) Thanks to AI, a lone threat actor was able to execute a cyber-attack that would have otherwise taken weeks in just 72 hours, according to a new report by Sygnia. The Israeli security vendor's reeport, Inside an AI-Assisted Cloud Attack: Familiar Techniques at Unfamiliar Speed, highlighted how the threat actor relied on AI for speed and scale, rather than researching novel malware or zero-day exploits. Using tried-and-tested techniques for attacking cloud infrastructure, an AWS environment was compromised with the goal of extortion, the report noted. [*Read more on agentic AI threats: Researchers Claim First Fully Agentic Ransomware - JadePuffer*](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/researchers-first-agentic/) The actor exploited control gaps in secrets management, identity governance, deployment workflows and cloud permissions, according to the report. They began by obtaining an access key to one of the AWS accounts through weaknesses in an internet-facing application. Then they used AI-assisted or agentic workflows for four concurrent tasks: * Searching for secrets and credentials to steal across various layers of the AWS environment. These included plaintext secrets stored in S3 buckets, API keys from application databases, secrets stored in AWS Secrets Manager, and parameters stored in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store * Creating backdoors and other persistence mechanisms such as creating new access keys and IAM users, establishing reverse shells on EC2 instances and ECS containers, and modifying deployment files * Exfiltrating data from RDS databases * Performing “impact actions” to demonstrate capability to the victim organization. These included denying access to S3 buckets, limiting ECS services or containers to a maximum capacity of zero, creating ACL rules to block network access, and purging SQS queues Crucially, the threat actor also benefitted from the organization’s gaps in visibility, monitoring, identity controls and incident preparedness, the report noted. ## **Remediation Advice for Network Defenders** Avi Dayan, VP of incident response at Sygnia, said the key takeaway for his team was the speed the threat actor moved post-intrusion and the volume of malicious activity they executed within a short timeframe. “This case underscores a growing challenge for defenders: as large language models and agentic AI become more accessible, they have the potential to lower the barrier to entry, accelerate attack workflows, and enable less sophisticated or resource-constrained threat actors to operate with unprecedented speed and scale,” he added. Sygnia recommended the following containment measures: * Restrict cloud management access through IP allowlisting and only permit access from trusted locations * Disable remote access VPN connectivity until containment steps are completed * Restrict outbound internet connectivity for workloads, servers, and cloud resources to approved destinations only * Apply firewall policies and network access control lists (ACLs) to block communication with known malicious infrastructure and restrict access to accidentally exposed assets * Enforce IP restrictions on source code repositories and development platforms * Route all app traffic through web application firewalls (WAFs) * Implement network segmentation and...

AI Agents.title: Talent in the age of co-intelligence: Why the next competitive edge is human, not digital - Consultancy-me.com
AI Agents.titleZh: Talent in the age of co-intelligence: Why the next competitive edge is human, not digital - Consultancy-me.com
AI Agents.content: # Talent in the age of co-intelligence: Why the next competitive edge is human, not digital 08 July 2026 Consultancy-me.com 3 min. read ***AI is reshaping economies faster than any technology before. Across the Middle East, organizations are deploying AI platforms, modernizing data infrastructure, and experimenting with generative tools at scale. Yet, beneath this momentum lies a less visible and far more consequential barrier: the reinvention gap.*** Increasingly, evidence suggests that technology is not the limiting factor. Talent is. The latest ‘[Pulse of Change](/news/12646/middle-east-organizations-ahead-in-ai-readiness-and-employee-adoption-confidence)’ report from [Accenture](/firms/accenture "Accenture") data reveals a 24-percentage-point gap between the change executives expect and the readiness employees feel. This is what we at Accenture call the ‘Reinvention Gap’ – the single greatest risk to the Middle East’s AI ambitions. ## The Shift to Co-intelligence Globally, most organizations now have access to powerful AI. What separates leaders from laggards is not infrastructure, but their transition from simple automation to co-intelligence. This is a new era where humans, AI agents, and robots redefine value together. In this model, AI doesn’t just support tasks; it can interpret intent, reason through options, and execute bounded workflows at speed. Humans must remain in the lead – setting direction, defining guardrails, making trade-offs, and owning outcomes. The organizations we call ‘Talent Reinventors’ – the leading 18% that combine AI adoption with a people-first mindset – are 6x more likely to create great employee experiences and significantly outperform peers in adaptability and innovation. For the Middle East, where workforce ambition and [AI readiness are already high](/news/12121/middle-east-companies-have-reinvention-strategies-in-place-yet-scaling-still-lags), this distinction is everything. ## The Middle East’s Leapfrog Opportunity Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Qatar have all made talent development a national priority. The region has a unique advantage: Middle Eastern employees are among the most confident in the world, with 73% ready for AI training – significantly higher than the 51% global average. AI Agents.title: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Is Dropping on Thursday: What's Different About Sol, Terra and Luna - CNET
AI Agents.titleZh: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Is Dropping on Thursday: What's Different About Sol, Terra and Luna - CNET
AI Agents.content: * [ai atlas](/ai-atlas/) * [Tech](/tech/) * Services & Software * [AI](/tech/services-and-software/ai/) # OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Is Dropping on Thursday: What's Different About Sol, Terra and Luna OpenAI says its flagship model for ChatGPT should make fewer mistakes. Katelyn Chedraoui Reporter 2 Katelyn is a reporter with CNET covering artificial intelligence, including chatbots, image and video generators. Her work explores how new AI technology is infiltrating our lives, shaping the content we consume on social media and affecting the people behind the screens. She graduated from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill with a degree in media and journalism. You can reach her at kchedraoui@cnet.com. Expertise artificial intelligence, AI image generators, social media platforms [See full bio](/profiles/kchedraoui/) Katelyn Chedraoui 2 min read OpenAI is getting ready to release its next generation of AI models. The GPT-5.6 series, which includes a trio of new models, will be released on Thursday, CEO Sam Altman confirmed [in a post](https://x.com/sama/status/2074709023807664454 "(opens in a new window)") on X Wednesday. The flagship model is called GPT-5.6 Sol. It's meant to handle deep reasoning tasks and manage agents. Terra is the middle, Goldilocks option in terms of capability and cost, while Luna is the quickest and cheapest to run. They are a "meaningful step up in cybersecurity capabilities" but also have advanced safety guardrails, the company [wrote](https://deploymentsafety.openai.com/gpt-5-6-preview "(opens in a new window)") when outlining the model family in June. OpenAI said Sol makes fewer factual errors, or hallucinations, compared to previous OpenAI models. We don't yet know the specific availability of these models, other than all three will be released on Thursday. CNET reached out and did not immediately receive a response from OpenAI. While we've known about GPT-5.6 [since its announcement on June 26](https://openai.com/index/previewing-gpt-5-6-sol/ "(opens in a new window)"), OpenAI has purposefully slow-rolled its release. Like [Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5](/tech/services-and-software/anthropic-project-glasswing-claude-mythos-preview/), the new GPT models are purportedly the most advanced AI available, and because of that, AI companies are being more hesitant with making them public. And for the first time, the US government is holding companies back with new requests for government review of AI models before they can be widely released. On June 2, President Donald Trump issued [an executive order](https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2026/06/promoting-advanced-artificial-intelligence-innovation-and-security/ "(opens in a new window)") requiring the Defense Department to design a system that has AI developers voluntarily sharing access to new frontier models with the government. Government officials have 30 days to review the tech and raise red flags. (This is a different legal standard than the one under which the government killed and then [resurrected Claude Fable 5](https://www.cnet.com/tech/services-and-software/anthropic-restores-claude-fable-5-government-export-control-news/).) OpenAI said that it had voluntarily given the government and some "trusted partners" early access. It's not clear who these partners are and what their expertise is, and OpenAI declined in June to comment on the specifics, referring CNET to the announcement. A White House official told CNET that the US government did not give OpenAI the green light to release GPT-5.6, nor was that required before a public release under the executive order. AI Agents.contentZh: * [ai atlas](/ai-atlas/) * [Tech](/tech/) * Services & Software * [AI](/tech/services-and-software/ai/) # OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Is Dropping on Thursday: What's Different About Sol, Terra and Luna OpenAI says its flagship model for ChatGPT should make fewer mistakes. Katelyn Chedraoui Reporter 2 Katelyn is a reporter with CNET covering artificial intelligence, including chatbots, image and video generators. Her work explores how new AI technology is infiltrating our lives, shaping the content we consume on social media and affecting the people behind the screens. She graduated from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill with a degree in media and journalism. You can reach her at kchedraoui@cnet.com. Expertise artificial intelligence, AI image generators, social media platforms [See full bio](/profiles/kchedraoui/) Katelyn Chedraoui 2 min read OpenAI is getting ready to release its next generation of AI models. The GPT-5.6 series, which includes a trio of new models, will be released on Thursday, CEO Sam Altman confirmed [in a post](https://x.com/sama/status/2074709023807664454 "(opens in a new window)") on X Wednesday. The flagship model is called GPT-5.6 Sol. It's meant to handle deep reasoning tasks and manage agents. Terra is the middle, Goldilocks option in terms of capability and cost, while Luna is the quickest and cheapest to run. They are a "meaningful step up in cybersecurity capabilities" but also have advanced safety guardrails, the company [wrote](https://deploymentsafety.openai.com/gpt-5-6-preview "(opens in a new window)") when outlining the model family in June. OpenAI said Sol makes fewer factual errors, or hallucinations, compared to previous OpenAI models. We don't yet know the specific availability of these models, other than all three will be released on Thursday. CNET reached out and did not immediately receive a response from OpenAI. While we've known about GPT-5.6 [since its announcement on June 26](https://openai.com/index/previewing-gpt-5-6-sol/ "(opens in a new window)"), OpenAI has purposefully slow-rolled its release. Like [Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5](/tech/services-and-software/anthropic-project-glasswing-claude-mythos-preview/), the new GPT models are purportedly the most advanced AI available, and because of that, AI companies are being more hesitant with making them public. And for the first time, the US government is holding companies back with new requests for government review of AI models before they can be widely released. On June 2, President Donald Trump issued [an executive order](https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2026/06/promoting-advanced-artificial-intelligence-innovation-and-security/ "(opens in a new window)") requiring the Defense Department to design a system that has AI developers voluntarily sharing access to new frontier models with the government. Government officials have 30 days to review the tech and raise red flags. (This is a different legal standard than the one under which the government killed and then [resurrected Claude Fable 5](https://www.cnet.com/tech/services-and-software/anthropic-restores-claude-fable-5-government-export-control-news/).) OpenAI said that it had voluntarily given the government and some "trusted partners" early access. It's not clear who these partners are and what their expertise is, and OpenAI declined in June to comment on the specifics, referring CNET to the announcement. A White House official told CNET that the US government did not give OpenAI the green light to release GPT-5.6, nor was that required before a public release under the executive order. They emphasized that all engagement is voluntarily. This is a big change in the government's stance on AI. The Trump administration had long been adamant that regulation would slow innovation and cause the US to "lose" the global AI race to China. Now, with government oversight on AI ramping up, it changes the pace of model release and the US's technological reputation on th...

LLMs.content: ### Topics [Biotech & Health](/category/biotech-health/) [Cloud Computing](/tag/cloud-computing/) [Crypto](/category/cryptocurrency/) [Enterprise](/category/enterprise/) [EVs](/tag/evs/) [Fintech](/category/fintech/) [Fundraising](/category/fundraising/) [Gadgets](/category/gadgets/) [Gaming](/category/gaming/) [Google](/tag/google/) [Government & Policy](/category/government-policy/) [Hardware](/category/hardware/) [Instagram](/tag/instagram/) [Layoffs](/tag/layoffs/) [Media & Entertainment](/category/media-entertainment/) [Meta](/tag/meta/) [Microsoft](/tag/microsoft/) [Privacy](/category/privacy/) [Robotics](/category/robotics/) [Security](/category/security/) [Social](/category/social/) [Space](/category/space/) [Startups](/category/startups/) [TikTok](/tag/tiktok/) [Transportation](/category/transportation/) [Venture](/category/venture/) ### More from TechCrunch [Staff](/about-techcrunch/) [Events](/events/) [Startup Battlefield](/startup-battlefield/) [StrictlyVC](https://strictlyvc.com/) [Newsletters](/newsletters/) [Podcasts](/podcasts/) [Videos](/video/) [Partner Content](/sponsored/) [TechCrunch Brand Studio](/brand-studio/) [Crunchboard](https://www.crunchboard.com/) [Contact Us](/contact-us/) [AI](https://techcrunch.com/category/artificial-intelligence/) # Hot French startup ZML releases free product to speed inference across lots of AI chips [Anna Heim](https://techcrunch.com/author/anna-heim/) The days of Nvidia’s unparalleled market dominance aren’t over, but challengers and choices are arising from all directions. [ZML](https://zml.ai/), a hot French AI startup [endorsed](https://x.com/ylecun/status/1836030233796874244) by Turing Award winner Yann LeCun, has released [inference](https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/03/artificial-intelligence-definition-glossary-hallucinations-guide-to-common-ai-terms/#inference)-performance software that allows [a variety of open-source large language models](https://x.com/steeve/status/2073166739668345255) to run on a [variety of chips](https://x.com/steeve/status/2058815921997680841) — including Nvidia’s, AMD’s, Google’s TPU, Apple Metal and Intel Arc. With [ZML/LLMD](https://zml.ai/llmd/), the newly launched LLM inference server, the company’s ambition is to break existing silos and make different chips available for AI use cases at their maximum available speed, and sometimes faster, ZML founder Steeve Morin told TechCrunch. As AI becomes integrated into our work and everyday lives, optimizing inference — aka, the processing of prompts — has been outpacing [model training](https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/03/artificial-intelligence-definition-glossary-hallucinations-guide-to-common-ai-terms/#training) in importance, but often feels patchy behind the scenes, with software and architecture barriers that lead to vendor lock-in, Morin said. The promise of achieving peak performance across a variety of chips is a technological feat, but it could also be a market disruptor, amid mounting fears over AI-related costs. ZML hopes to provide enterprises and clouds with the option to use a mix of chips, some of which might be less costly or consume less energy. “The idea is to give people back the power to create their own system and achieve real efficiency gains that allow [AI] to be disseminated,” Morin said. Such a software assist may help novel AI chipmakers, many of which happen to be from Europe, Morin observed, citing [Axelera](https://axelera.ai/), [Fractile](https://www.fractile.ai/), [Kalray](https://www.kalrayinc.com/), [OLIX](https://olix.com/), [Q.ANT](https://qant.com/), [SiPearl](https://sipearl.com/), [SpiNNcloud](https://spinncloud.com/), and [VSORA](https://vsora.com/). But more than their region of origin, what matters to him is that ZML can work with them on “things that haven’t been done before anywhere in the world.” That doesn’t mean Morin is bearish on Nvidia. [He’s not](https://www.thetwentyminutevc.com/steeve-morin), in part because of its existing supply. He told Tec...

Report us") Related Topics [OpenAI](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/OpenAI "OpenAI") [ChatGPT](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/ChatGPT "ChatGPT") [OpenAI](https://www.ynetnews.com/business/article/bkfyaro11ml) will publicly launch its most capable model, GPT-5.6, on Thursday after delaying the release last month at the request of the U.S. government amid national security concerns over the potential misuse of powerful AI systems. The launch comes after the U.S. government lifted restrictions on Anthropic’s latest Fable and Mythos AI models last week, less than three weeks after the company was ordered to suspend access to them over national security concerns. Washington has increased scrutiny of advanced AI model releases as officials seek to identify potential threats, amid concerns that the technology could be misused by military or intelligence agencies in China, Russia or other countries of concern. Axios, which first reported the OpenAI launch, said a source familiar with the matter told the outlet that the U.S. Department of Commerce approved a broad release of GPT-5.6 following additional government testing under Washington’s new oversight framework for frontier AI models. OpenAI had previously limited access to the model to a small group of vetted partners whose details were shared with U.S. authorities. The company said in a post on X late Tuesday that it plans to launch GPT-5.6 Sol, along with two additional models, Terra and Luna. Sol is OpenAI’s most advanced model to date, while Terra is a mid-tier, lower-cost model and Luna is the company’s most cost-efficient option. The White House and the US Department of Commerce did not immediately respond to Reuters requests for comment outside regular business hours. The increased scrutiny of AI models began after U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order establishing a voluntary framework requiring developers of “covered frontier models” to provide the government with access to their syst...

LLMs.title: OpenAI's most advanced AI model is breaking free — and colliding with Elon Musk's latest Grok release - Business Insider
LLMs.titleZh: OpenAI's most advanced AI model is breaking free — and colliding with Elon Musk's latest Grok release - Business Insider
LLMs.content: [Subscribe](https://www.businessinsider.com/subscription) [Newsletters](https://www.businessinsider.com/subscription/newsletter) [Tech](/tech) # OpenAI's most advanced AI model is breaking free — and colliding with Elon Musk's latest Grok release By [Hugh Langley](https://www.businessinsider.com/author/hugh-langley) You're currently following this author! Want to unfollow? Unsubscribe via the link in your email. [Read in app](https://insider-app.onelink.me/4cpG/?af_js_web=true&af_ss_ver=2_3_0&af_dp=insider%3A%2F%2Fbi%2Fpost%2Fopenai-sol-terra-luna-gpt56-grok-elon-musk-sam-altman-2026-7&af_force_deeplink=true&is_retargeting=true&deep_link_value=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.businessinsider.com%2Fopenai-sol-terra-luna-gpt56-grok-elon-musk-sam-altman-2026-7&pid=businessinsider&c=post_page_share_bar_v2_smart_4.13.23 "Download the app") Copy link [Email](mailto:?subject=OpenAI's most advanced AI model is breaking free — and colliding with Elon Musk's latest Grok release&body=OpenAI's%20most%20advanced%20AI%20model%20is%20breaking%20free%20%E2%80%94%20and%20colliding%20with%20Elon%20Musk's%20latest%20Grok%20release%0D%0A%0D%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fwww.businessinsider.com%2Fopenai-sol-terra-luna-gpt56-grok-elon-musk-sam-altman-2026-7& "Email") Facebook WhatsApp X LinkedIn Bluesky Threads Impact Link Save Saved This story is available exclusively to Business Insider subscribers. [Become an Insider](/subscription) and start reading now. Have an account? . OpenAI's latest and most powerful AI model is finally getting a public rollout, but not without Elon Musk stealing a bit of thunder. OpenAI said it will roll out its new GPT-5.6 family of models more widely on Thursday, following the Trump administration's request that the company stagger the model's release. A few hours after OpenAI's announcement, which came late Tuesday California time, Musk said that SpaceXAI would launch its new Grok 4.5 model in the coming days. OpenAI [said last month](https://openai.com/index/previewing-gpt-5-6-sol/) that it had made a version of GPT-5.6 available to a limited set of partners who would provide feedback ahead of a general release. That announcement followed a clash between [Anthropic and the Trump administration](https://www.businessinsider.com/anthropic-white-house-fable-mythos-5-drama-explained-2026-6), which put an export ban on the company's Fable and Mythos models, citing the risk that they could be misused by adversaries for cyberattacks. In response,Anthropic suspended the models. Fable 5 was [made available again last week](https://www.anthropic.com/news/redeploying-fable-5). OpenAI's new GPT-5.6 will come in three flavors: Sol, Terra, and Luna. Sol is the flagship model, which OpenAI says has stronger agentic abilities in coding, biology, and cybersecurity. OpenAI said Terra is better "for everyday work," while Luna is focused on speed and affordability. Meanwhile,Musk said that Grok 4.5 is an "Opus-class model, but faster, more token-efficient and lower cost." Opus is Anthropic's family of advanced AI models. [### Explore BI Games Take a smarter break in your day - and see how far you get. Play now](/games) The dueling announcements set the stage for another chapter in the rivalry between Musk and OpenAI CEO SamAltman, who have just come out of a fierce legal battle. Musk, who cofounded OpenAI but later stepped away, sued OpenAI and its leaders in 2024, accusing them of betraying an agreement that OpenAI would stay a nonprofit. The case went to trial earlier this year, and [Musk ultimately lost](https://www.businessinsider.com/openai-sam-altman-elon-musk-jury-trial-verdict-2026-5), with the jury ruling he had waited too long to file a lawsuit. It's also notable that both OpenAI and Musk are highlighting efficiency as a selling point for some of their new models. As companies are becoming conscious of their token spend, labs are trying to make models [faster and cheaper to run](https://www.businessinsider.com/google-ai-cost-tokens-gemini-flash-openai-anth...

LLMs.title: New Name, New Grok: SpaceXAI Officially Ties Its Brand to the World's Most Problematic Chatbot - Gizmodo
LLMs.titleZh: New Name, New Grok: SpaceXAI Officially Ties Its Brand to the World's Most Problematic Chatbot - Gizmodo
LLMs.content: [Skip to content](#main) [Artificial Intelligence](https://gizmodo.com/tech/artificial-intelligence) # New Name, New Grok: SpaceXAI Officially Ties Its Brand to the World’s Most Problematic Chatbot Good luck with that. By [AJ Dellinger](https://gizmodo.com/author/ajdellinger1) Reading time 2 minutes [Comments (0)](https://gizmodo.com/new-name-new-grok-spacexai-officially-ties-its-brand-to-the-worlds-most-problematic-chatbot-2000783130#comments) The federal government managed to temporarily ban Anthropic’s latest AI model from being used by foreign nationals and [reportedly held up the release](https://www.foxnews.com/science/trump-puts-brakes-openais-newest-ai-model) of OpenAI’s updated GPT-5.6 Sol for security concerns, but it seems the latest version of Grok must have just skated right through. Grok 4.5, the first release of an AI model under the newly formed SpaceXAI, was [made available to the public on Wednesday](https://x.ai/news/grok-4-5). Elon Musk’s rocket/social media/AI company claims Grok 4.5 is its “smartest model built to excel at coding, agentic tasks, and knowledge work.” Notably, it’s also the first release the company has made since partnering with AI coding company Cursor. In particular, SpaceXAI said its latest model “excels at real engineering tasks” and is “equally adept at office work,” and claimed it compares favorably to other leading models in those areas. Despite that, Grok still seems behind on most major benchmarks. It trails both Claude’s Fable 5—the “safe” version of its super-powerful Mythos model—and OpenAI’s GPT-5.5, which is now no longer that company’s flagship option, anyway. In most of the testing metrics, Grok 4.5 scores on par with Anthropic’s Opus 4.8, which is a couple of months old at this point. The calling card for Grok, though, appears to be efficiency and cost. “It is an Opus-class model, but faster, more token-efficient, and lower cost,” Musk wrote in a [post on X](https://x.com/elonmusk/status/2074740539874775163). To that end, the company claims that Grok 4.5 costs about $2 per one million input tokens and $6 per one million output tokens. By comparison, [according to The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/grok-4-5-is-so-cheap-compared-to-fable-5-and-gpt-5-5-that-benchmark-gaps-may-not-matter-much/), Opus 4.8 runs at $5 for inputs and $25 for outputs. Fable, Anthropic’s big hitter, costs $10 for input and $50 for output on that same one-million-token scale. “Cheap” probably isn’t a word anyone would typically associate with SpaceX, but, in general, Grok is kind of a square peg in a round hole. SpaceX has managed to [keep itself clean of the Elon Musk stink](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/brink-ipo-musks-spacex-already-100308599.html) even as he drives his own personal brand into the ground, but associating with Grok is going to be a real test of the brand’s strength. It’s tough to get roped into being linked to an AI model that is at this point best known for producing non-consensual nude images, [including those of children](https://gizmodo.com/people-spent-the-holidays-asking-grok-to-generate-sexual-images-of-children-2000704919), at a scale never before achieved—a pretty dubious honor if ever there was one. Since Grok seemingly didn’t get caught up by government review, Grok 4.5 is available in Grok Build, all Cursor plans, and from the SpaceXAI console starting today. Report us") Related Topics [AI](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/AI "AI") [Startup](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Startup "Startup") The rapid growth of generative AI has created one of the fastest-expanding technology markets in history, but Israeli startup Velocity believes the next major challenge will not be building AI products, but finding ways to monetize and distribute them at scale. The company announced Tuesday that it has raised $27 million in seed funding in a round led by NFX and Red Dot Capital Partners, with participation from Stardom Ventures, Corner Ventures and Transcend. Velocity was founded by CEO Tal Shoham alongside COO Amir Shaked and CPO Nimrod Zuta, former executives at ironSource and Unity who spent more than a decade building monetization, advertising and growth infrastructure used by developers worldwide. Velocity is developing infrastructure for AI-native applications as companies face two growing challenges: rising costs of running advanced AI models and difficulty converting large numbers of free users into paying subscribers. While hundreds of millions of people use AI-powered products, including assistants, coding tools and specialized applications, only a small percentage become paying customers. The company argues that traditional subscription models alone may not be enough and is building a monetization layer based on AI-native advertising and recommendations integrated directly into user conversations. Instead of limiting users of an AI photo-editing tool to a small number of free images before requiring payment, Velocity’s technology could allow more free usage by generating revenue from advertisements based on real-time user needs. At the center of the platform is what Velocity calls “user intent” — the information people reveal when they interact with AI systems about what they want to learn, create, buy or solve. AI Product.content: ### Topics [Biotech & Health](/category/biotech-health/) [Cloud Computing](/tag/cloud-computing/) [Crypto](/category/cryptocurrency/) [Enterprise](/category/enterprise/) [EVs](/tag/evs/) [Fintech](/category/fintech/) [Fundraising](/category/fundraising/) [Gadgets](/category/gadgets/) [Gaming](/category/gaming/) [Google](/tag/google/) [Government & Policy](/category/government-policy/) [Hardware](/category/hardware/) [Instagram](/tag/instagram/) [Layoffs](/tag/layoffs/) [Media & Entertainment](/category/media-entertainment/) [Meta](/tag/meta/) [Microsoft](/tag/microsoft/) [Privacy](/category/privacy/) [Robotics](/category/robotics/) [Security](/category/security/) [Social](/category/social/) [Space](/category/space/) [Startups](/category/startups/) [TikTok](/tag/tiktok/) [Transportation](/category/transportation/) [Venture](/category/venture/) ### More from TechCrunch [Staff](/about-techcrunch/) [Events](/events/) [Startup Battlefield](/startup-battlefield/) [StrictlyVC](https://strictlyvc.com/) [Newsletters](/newsletters/) [Podcasts](/podcasts/) [Videos](/video/) [Partner Content](/sponsored/) [TechCrunch Brand Studio](/brand-studio/) [Crunchboard](https://www.crunchboard.com/) [Contact Us](/contact-us/) [AI](https://techcrunch.com/category/artificial-intelligence/) # Prime Intellect raises $130M Series A to help enterprises build their own AI agents [Marina Temkin](https://techcrunch.com/author/marina-temkin/) Prime Intellect, a startup that provides computing power and specialized software tools that help companies build AI agents, has raised $130 million Series A at a $1 billion valuation. The massive round was led by Radical Ventures, with participation from Nvidia Ventures, Intel Capital, Dell Technologies Capital, Iconiq, and a long list of angel investors who are founders of notable companies, including, Aravind Srinivas (Perplexity), Aaron Levie (Box), Winston Weinberg (Harvey), Jeff Wang (Cognition), and Brendan Foody (Mercor). Founded in 2024, Prime Intellect’s goal is to give organizations capabilities to train their own agentic systems without relying on frontier AI labs. While this mission would have been hard to achieve just a few years ago, the rise of reinforcement learning techniques, which iteratively reward successful task completion and penalizes errors, can allow companies to become their “own AI lab” by refining models for specific business tasks. Although it is now possible to bypass closed AI labs, the underlying infrastructure remains so complex that most companies lack the expertise to assemble these pieces into a production-ready system. That’s where Prime Intellect comes in. The startup has developed what it calls a “full-stack” for AI agent development, which includes compute access, a reinforcement learning framework, and evaluation tools. Prime Intellect’s platform functions like a marketplace, providing modular access so customers can pick and choose the specific tools they need without being locked into an all-or-nothing system. “They’ve stitched this together and built it in such a way that they’re operating at the frontier in a way that’s affordable,” said David Katz, a partner at Radical Ventures. He added that while others offer bits and pieces, Prime Intellect is unique in providing the capabilities of a top-tier AI lab as a “one-stop shop” for development. The startup’s approach has attracted customers like Ramp, Zapier, and Flapping Airplanes, who pay the startup for a hosted version of its tools. This rapid adoption has propelled the company to an annualized revenue run rate of $100 million. This growth is driven by the tangible results. For example, Ramp used Prime Intellect to build an agent that helped the fintech find answers inside spreadsheets. “The result beat the frontier models on accuracy while running at faster speeds and a fraction of the cost,” Ramp’s co-founder and co-CEO Karim Atiyeh said in a statement. AI Product.titleZh: Prime Intellect raises $130M for AI training platform - Let's Data Science
AI Product.content: [Skip to content](#main-content) [LDS Let's Data ScienceLEARN • BUILD • STAY AHEAD](/) [Sign in](/login?redirect=%2Fnews%2Fprime-intellect-raises-130m-for-ai-training-platform-44f35cad) Funding & Businessreinforcement learninginfrastructureprime intellectseries a # Prime Intellect raises $130M for AI training platform ||By [LDS Team](/editorial-standards) 7.6 Relevance Score Enterprises building agentic systems face high integration and compute costs; turnkey training stacks lower the barrier to in-house model refinement. Reporting: **Prime Intellect** closed a **$130 million** Series A at a **$1 billion** valuation, with Radical Ventures listed as lead and participation from Nvidia Ventures, Intel Capital, Dell Technologies Capital and Iconiq, according to TechCrunch and Axios. SiliconANGLE and TechCrunch describe the company as offering a managed training platform and a "full stack" for agent development that includes the open-source toolkits **Verifiers** and **Prime-RL**, plus managed GPU clusters and deployment infrastructure. AIWeekly and aVenture report a **$100 million** annualized run rate for the company but flag the figure as reported and not audited. ### Editorial analysis - practitioner significance Companies attempting to build production agentic systems confront three recurring problems: sandboxed environment construction, scalable RL-based training orchestration, and predictable compute economics. A managed, modular training stack reduces integration overhead for engineering teams and shifts tradeoffs from building plumbing toward model and evaluation engineering. This is a sector-level pattern rather than a claim about any single firm. ### What happened TechCrunch and Axios report that **Prime Intellect** raised **$130 million** in a Series A round valuing the company at **$1 billion**. TechCrunch and Axios identify **Radical Ventures** as the lead investor, with participation from **Nvidia Ventures**, **Intel Capital**, **Dell Technologies Capital** and **Iconiq**, and a roster of angel backers, per their coverage. SiliconANGLE reports that Prime Intellect operates a cloud platform optimized for customizing open-source models and that the company released two open-source toolkits, **Verifiers** and **Prime-RL**, last year. SiliconANGLE also reports the platform parallelizes training across large GPU clusters and relies on PyTorch's **FSDP2** for distributed memory efficiency, while supporting parameter-efficient approaches such as **LoRA**. AIWeekly and aVenture report an annualized revenue run rate of **$100 million**, noting that the figure is reported and not audited. ### Editorial analysis - technical context Reinforcement-learning-driven agent training commonly requires realistic, instrumented sandboxes and scalable orchestration to collect rewards and transitions at production scale. Industry-pattern observations: companies that package sandbox generation and RL orchestration reduce ramp time for domain teams but inherit operational risks around reproducibility, cost predictability, and evaluation fidelity. Open-source contributions like **Prime-RL** and use of FSDP2 are technically notable because they indicate an emphasis on memory-efficient, large-scale parallelism; practitioners should treat these as signals about the platform's intended scale and engineering tradeoffs, not as independent validation of model quality. ### Context and significance Reporting shows major hardware and infrastructure investors are participating in the round, which aligns with a broader pattern where chip vendors and cloud-adjacent investors back software layers that drive GPU utilization. Industry context: when compute and orchestration vendors capture enterprise workloads, buyer conversations shift from pure model accuracy to total cost of ownership, deployment portability, and evaluation tooling. AI Product.title: Exclusive: OpenAI deployment arm to acquire Northslope - Axios
AI Product.titleZh: Exclusive: OpenAI deployment arm to acquire Northslope - Axios
AI Product.content: [Skip to main content](#main-content) [Axios](/) 39 mins ago - [Technology](https://www.axios.com/technology) # Exclusive: OpenAI deployment arm to acquire Northslope * [Madison Mills](https://www.axios.com/authors/mmills) [Add Axios on Google](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=axios.com) Add Axios as your preferred source to see more of our stories on Google. [Add Axios on Google](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=axios.com) The OpenAI Deployment Company agreed to acquire Northslope, an applied AI firm, OpenAI shared exclusively with Axios, marking its second acquisition focused on enterprise AI use since launch. **The big picture:** AI companies are starting to do work that was traditionally left to consulting firms, betting that helping customers implement AI will be as important as building the models themselves. **Catch up quick:** The OpenAI Deployment Company launched in May as part of OpenAI's effort to help enterprises deploy AI in core business operations. * Northslope is the Deployment Company's second acquisition, following AI deployment firm Tomoro. * The deal expands the Deployment Company's bench to hundreds of "forward deployed engineers" (FDEs) who work alongside customers to build AI systems inside their organizations. * OpenAI's deployment arm, which is majority-owned and controlled by OpenAI, kicked off with $4 billion under its belt to fund acquisitions, but the terms of this deal weren't disclosed, and it is subject to customary regulatory approvals. **Between the lines:** As frontier models become increasingly comparable, it's harder to win on model performance alone. Enterprises need to know how to use the tools. * FDEs have the benefit of speaking both tech and business languages, helping bridge the gaps between teams and employees who want to use AI models for certain tasks but may struggle to prompt solutions themselves. * That strategy mirrors Palantir's long-standing approach of embedding engineers directly with customers to build software around their operations. (Northslope's founders come from Palantir.) * It's not just OpenAI: [Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/news/enterprise-ai-services-company) is building an AI services company to help mid-sized businesses use Claude. **What we're watching:** Whether FDEs can keep enterprises adopting and scaling their AI usage amid concern about AI spend, enterprise IP and security overall. **The bottom line:** The next phase of the AI race may be defined by who can get businesses to use their AI tools rather than model releases. [Add Axios on Google](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=axios.com) ##### What to read next

Open Source AI.titleZh: Hot French startup ZML releases free product to speed inference across lots of AI chips - TechCrunch
Open Source AI.content: ### Topics [Biotech & Health](/category/biotech-health/) [Cloud Computing](/tag/cloud-computing/) [Crypto](/category/cryptocurrency/) [Enterprise](/category/enterprise/) [EVs](/tag/evs/) [Fintech](/category/fintech/) [Fundraising](/category/fundraising/) [Gadgets](/category/gadgets/) [Gaming](/category/gaming/) [Google](/tag/google/) [Government & Policy](/category/government-policy/) [Hardware](/category/hardware/) [Instagram](/tag/instagram/) [Layoffs](/tag/layoffs/) [Media & Entertainment](/category/media-entertainment/) [Meta](/tag/meta/) [Microsoft](/tag/microsoft/) [Privacy](/category/privacy/) [Robotics](/category/robotics/) [Security](/category/security/) [Social](/category/social/) [Space](/category/space/) [Startups](/category/startups/) [TikTok](/tag/tiktok/) [Transportation](/category/transportation/) [Venture](/category/venture/) ### More from TechCrunch [Staff](/about-techcrunch/) [Events](/events/) [Startup Battlefield](/startup-battlefield/) [StrictlyVC](https://strictlyvc.com/) [Newsletters](/newsletters/) [Podcasts](/podcasts/) [Videos](/video/) [Partner Content](/sponsored/) [TechCrunch Brand Studio](/brand-studio/) [Crunchboard](https://www.crunchboard.com/) [Contact Us](/contact-us/) [AI](https://techcrunch.com/category/artificial-intelligence/) # Hot French startup ZML releases free product to speed inference across lots of AI chips [Anna Heim](https://techcrunch.com/author/anna-heim/) The days of Nvidia’s unparalleled market dominance aren’t over, but challengers and choices are arising from all directions. [ZML](https://zml.ai/), a hot French AI startup [endorsed](https://x.com/ylecun/status/1836030233796874244) by Turing Award winner Yann LeCun, has released [inference](https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/03/artificial-intelligence-definition-glossary-hallucinations-guide-to-common-ai-terms/#inference)-performance software that allows [a variety of open-source large language models](https://x.com/steeve/status/2073166739668345255) to run on a [variety of chips](https://x.com/steeve/status/2058815921997680841) — including Nvidia’s, AMD’s, Google’s TPU, Apple Metal and Intel Arc. With [ZML/LLMD](https://zml.ai/llmd/), the newly launched LLM inference server, the company’s ambition is to break existing silos and make different chips available for AI use cases at their maximum available speed, and sometimes faster, ZML founder Steeve Morin told TechCrunch. As AI becomes integrated into our work and everyday lives, optimizing inference — aka, the processing of prompts — has been outpacing [model training](https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/03/artificial-intelligence-definition-glossary-hallucinations-guide-to-common-ai-terms/#training) in importance, but often feels patchy behind the scenes, with software and architecture barriers that lead to vendor lock-in, Morin said. The promise of achieving peak performance across a variety of chips is a technological feat, but it could also be a market disruptor, amid mounting fears over AI-related costs. ZML hopes to provide enterprises and clouds with the option to use a mix of chips, some of which might be less costly or consume less energy. “The idea is to give people back the power to create their own system and achieve real efficiency gains that allow [AI] to be disseminated,” Morin said. Such a software assist may help novel AI chipmakers, many of which happen to be from Europe, Morin observed, citing [Axelera](https://axelera.ai/), [Fractile](https://www.fractile.ai/), [Kalray](https://www.kalrayinc.com/), [OLIX](https://olix.com/), [Q.ANT](https://qant.com/), [SiPearl](https://sipearl.com/), [SpiNNcloud](https://spinncloud.com/), and [VSORA](https://vsora.com/). But more than their region of origin, what matters to him is that ZML can work with them on “things that haven’t been done before anywhere in the world.” That doesn’t mean Morin is bearish on Nvidia. [He’s not](https://www.thetwentyminutevc.com/steeve-morin), in part because of its existing supply. He told Tec...

Open Source AI.content: [Asosiy kontentga oʻtish](#main) * [Uzbekistan](/en/uzbekistan/) * [World](/en/world/) * [Society](/en/society/) * [Tech](/en/technology/) * [Sport](/en/sport/) * [Auto](/en/auto/) * [Culture](/en/culture/) * [Economy](/en/economy/) * [Ўзбекча](#) * [O'zbek](#) * [Русский](#) * [Türkçe](#) * [Қазақша](#) * [Français](#) * [Español](#) * [Deutsch](#) # French startup ZML challenges NVIDIA's hegemony in the AI chip market ·1·[Technology](/en/technology/) In an era of rapid AI development, [NVIDIA](/en/tags/nvidia/)'s absolute market dominance is facing a serious challenge. French startup ZML has introduced new software backed by Turing Award winner Yann LeCun. This product allows AI models to run at maximum speed on chips from various manufacturers. This was reported by Techcrunch.com [reports](https://zamin.uz/index.php?do=go&url=aHR0cHM6Ly90ZWNoY3J1bmNoLmNvbS8yMDI2LzA3LzA4L2hvdC1mcmVuY2gtc3RhcnR1cC16bWwtcmVsZWFzZXMtZnJlZS1wcm9kdWN0LXRvLXNwZWVkLWluZmVyZW5jZS1hY3Jvc3MtbG90cy1vZi1haS1jaGlwcy8%3D) . According to TechCrunch, the new inference server called ZML/LLMD is designed to run open-source large language models (LLM) efficiently on various processors, not just NVIDIA, but also AMD, Google TPU, Apple Metal, and Intel Arc. This [technology](/en/tags/tehnologiya/) allows companies to avoid vendor lock-in and fully utilize existing hardware resources. ## A revolution in the inference process Currently, using ready-made AI models, i.e., the inference process, is becoming more important than training AI. ZML founder Steeve Morin explains that many companies are dependent on specific [chip](/en/tags/chip/) manufacturers due to software and architectural barriers. The new platform removes these barriers and unlocks the maximum potential of the chips. This solution is particularly relevant given the rising costs and energy consumption of AI. The ZML startup enables enterprises to use combinations of cheaper or more energy-efficient chips. This, in turn, makes the implementation of AI systems more accessible and economically efficient. ## New opportunities for European chips The software introduced by ZML is expected to be a major boost not only for giants but also for emerging chip manufacturers. In his interview, Morin listed several promising companies: * Axelera and Fractile; * Kalray and OLIX; * SiPearl and SpiNNcloud; * Q.ANT and VSORA. The startup leader emphasized that his relationship with NVIDIA is good and that this project is not against them, but rather serves to expand the market. Nevertheless, ZML currently faces competition from major players like Baseten, Inferact, and RadixArk. The Paris-based team of just 20 people has now reached the stage of co-designing silicon. This news is also important for specialists and local IT companies. Open-source models running on various chips allow for the optimization of computing power and cost reduction. This step by the ZML startup signals the beginning of an 'inference gold rush' in the AI market.
